old_claim.note: Urban Water Chlorination provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The early sanitary inquiries compiled disease returns, local medical reports, inspections, and comparative facts, making public-health data collection and analysis a direct enabling method.

source_shape.source_locator: Prefatory letter (pp. iii-x) and Appendix instructions 1-4 (pp. xi-xvi) record the 1838 metropolitan inquiries and prescribe disease returns, institutional data, local medical reports, personal examination, and comparison of collected statements with other authentic facts.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The founding sanitary inquiries used organized public-health data and inspection methods decades before municipal chlorination.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Public-health statistics supplied the inquiry method directly documented in 1838-1842, whereas urban water chlorination began much later and is one possible intervention that surveys could evaluate rather than a prerequisite for conducting them.

ontology_before: The survey node depended on a 1908 water-treatment intervention, reversing the historical sequence between sanitary investigation and later municipal treatment.
ontology_after: The survey node depends on the documented health-data method and leaves chlorination as a later intervention outside its direct prerequisite boundary.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-urban-sanitary-surveys-boundary: public_health_statistics is a direct enabler and urban_water_chlorination is absent.
would_reject_if: Primary evidence establishes that the scoped sanitary-survey practice began only after municipal chlorination and could not be conducted without it.
short_reason: The 1838-1842 inquiries explicitly used disease returns and comparative public-health data, while chlorination followed decades later.
